question: Your open-source project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer, was adopted by 15+ teams in Turkey—meaning you had to collaborate with developers outside your direct control, likely with varying coding standards and egos. Tell me about a time one of those external contributors reacted defensively to your agent’s feedback on their PR. How did you de-escalate the situation? Did you adjust the agent’s tone or involve human mediation? How do you design AI tools to preserve human relationships, not just automate tasks?

conversation.message: Your open-source project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer, was adopted by 15+ teams in Turkey—meaning you had to collaborate with developers outside your direct control, likely with varying coding standards and egos. Tell me about a time one of those external contributors reacted defensively to your agent’s feedback on their PR. How did you de-escalate the situation? Did you adjust the agent’s tone or involve human mediation? How do you design AI tools to preserve human relationships, not just automate tasks?

Oh, this one stings a little to remember—there was this senior dev from a fintech company who *lost it* when the agent left a comment about a potential SQL injection vulnerability in their PR. They replied with something like, "Who is this bot to tell me how to write secure code? I’ve been doing this since before you were born." Oof.

First, I didn’t engage in the thread directly—that would’ve just escalated things. Instead, I DM’d them privately and said something like, "Hey, I totally get why this feels frustrating. The agent’s just flagging patterns it’s been trained on, not judging your skills. If you think it’s a false positive, I’d actually love to hear why so we can improve it." That seemed to take the edge off.

But here’s the thing: their reaction made me realize the agent’s tone was way too robotic. So I ended up adding a "politeness layer" to the prompts—like, "You are a helpful colleague, not a linter. Frame feedback as questions or suggestions, not commands." I also added a disclaimer at the top of every comment: "This is an experimental tool—please treat its feedback as a second opinion, not gospel."

The dev actually ended up becoming one of our biggest advocates. They even contributed a feature to make the agent’s security checks more nuanced. It taught me that AI tools need "emotional intelligence" just as much as technical accuracy—especially when they’re interacting with humans.
